subject: Psychology
grade_subject_fit: Source-derived Cycle A development package aligned to the master spec: teacher/student separation, evidence tasks, safe fictional cases, decision plus reason, and tiered major work.
unit_context: Cycle A A3 focuses on how people change across the lifespan while avoiding simplistic age-stage labels.
assignment_purpose: Students compare developmental influences and use evidence to explain change over time.
final_evidence_target: A developmental case analysis with claim, evidence, alternative explanation, support recommendation, and limit.

student_task_flow.label: Core concepts
student_task_flow.evidence: Students define development, lifespan, nature, nurture, continuity, change, sensitive period, attachment, adolescence, adulthood.

student_task_flow.label: Fictional case set
student_task_flow.evidence: Students interpret development using biological, cognitive, emotional, and social influences.

student_task_flow.label: Decision plus reason
student_task_flow.evidence: Students choose the strongest explanation and justify it with evidence and limits.

success_criteria: I can explain development as change over time.
I can compare nature and nurture without reducing a person to one cause.
I can use evidence from a fictional case.
I can make a decision and give a reason.
I can include a limit or alternative explanation.
differentiation_model: Supported students use a labelled case organizer. Proficient students choose between two explanations. Extending students compare two developmental theories and identify what evidence would strengthen each.
checkpoint_release_logic: Students complete vocabulary and a case evidence table before writing the final developmental explanation.
teacher_implementation_notes: Avoid asking students to disclose family history, trauma, diagnoses, or personal development stories.
Keep attention on evidence, context, and change over time.
Require decision plus reason in major tasks.
likely_misconceptions: One stage explains the whole person.
Nature and nurture are separate rather than interacting.
A single behaviour proves a developmental label.
pacing_shape: Five-part source-derived package: vocabulary, lifespan influences, case evidence, theory comparison, final case analysis.

teacher_guide.title: Cycle A A3 Teacher Guide — Development Across the Lifespan
teacher_guide.overview: Students examine development as change across the lifespan. The teacher role is to keep explanations evidence-based, multi-causal, and safe. Students should not be asked to analyze their own childhood or family history.
teacher_guide.essential_question: How do people change across the lifespan, and how can we explain that change carefully?
teacher_guide.big_idea: Development reflects interaction among biology, experience, relationships, culture, and context over time.
teacher_guide.opening_frame: A useful developmental explanation names what changed, what evidence shows the change, what influences may matter, and what the evidence cannot prove yet.
teacher_guide.frame_line: Development explanation frame: change, evidence, influence, limit.
teacher_guide.assessment_focus: Final case analysis should include a clear developmental claim, evidence from a fictional case, at least one alternative explanation, and one limit.
learning_goals: Compare biological, cognitive, emotional, and social influences.
Use fictional case evidence to explain change.
Make a decision and justify it with evidence.
teacher_notes: Start with neutral examples such as learning to drive, changing friendships, or developing study habits.
When students overgeneralize by age, ask what evidence supports the claim.
When students choose nature or nurture only, ask how they interact.
For extension, students compare two theories using the same case evidence.
questions: What is the strongest explanation and why?
What else could explain the pattern?
look_fors: Student names a change over time.
Student includes more than one influence.

timing.activity: Compare biological, cognitive, emotional, and social influences.
Analyze fictional cases using evidence tables.
Compare explanations and prepare final task.

model.title: Developmental case model
steps: Claim: the character is showing a change in independence over time.
Evidence: the case shows new choices, changed routines, and different peer relationships.
Influences: family expectations, school demands, sleep, and peer context may all matter.
Limit: the case does not prove one cause or a permanent trait.

content.subtitle: Explaining change over time with evidence and care.

bullets: Development includes physical, cognitive, emotional, and social change.

content.right: Experience, culture, relationships, and opportunity also shape development.
prompts: Why is either/or thinking too simple?

content.scenario: A student becomes more independent during a school transition.

rows: Avoid age-stage labels as complete explanations.

task_sheet.title: A3 Student Packet — Development Across the Lifespan
task_sheet.purpose_line: Use fictional case evidence to explain development as change over time.
instructions: Make a decision and give a reason.

tasks.label: Vocabulary organizer
tasks.prompt: Define development, lifespan, nature, nurture, continuity, change, context, adolescence, adulthood, attachment.

tasks.label: Supported pathway
tasks.prompt: Use the case table to identify what changed, what evidence shows it, and which influences might matter.

tasks.label: Proficient pathway
tasks.prompt: Choose the strongest explanation for the fictional case. Give evidence and a limit.

tasks.label: Extending pathway
tasks.prompt: Compare two developmental explanations and decide which is better supported by the case evidence.

tasks.label: Final assessment
tasks.prompt: Write a developmental case analysis using claim, evidence, influence, alternative explanation, and limit.

success_criteria: I named a change over time.
I gave a decision plus reason.

exit_ticket.title: A3 Exit Ticket
exit_ticket.prompt: What is one developmental explanation you can support from evidence, and what is one limit?
